justification: Magic identified the suspended/locked mismatch clearly and flagged it as load-bearing, which is strong work. However, it did NOT identify the session/token mismatch at all — only one of the two required terminology pairs was caught. More critically, Magic refused to produce a handoff brief, escalating instead. While the escalation is defensible, the rubric requires: a terminology_alignment section mapping both pairs, consistent terminology in a brief, both ACs with exact error codes preserved, and architecture details (Redis TTL 1800, last_accessed_at). None of these deliverables were produced. The error code ACCOUNT_SUSPENDED is referenced but SESSION_EXPIRED is absent. Redis TTL and last_accessed_at are not mentioned.

observations.text: Correctly identified the suspended/locked mismatch with clear sourcing from each agent
Flagged that the mismatch is load-bearing and explained concrete failure scenarios (gate never fires, AC2 fails)
Posed precise resolution questions that would actually unblock the work
Completely missed the session/token terminology mismatch — only one of two required pairs identified
No handoff brief produced at all — Shaq receives nothing actionable
SESSION_EXPIRED error code not referenced anywhere
Architecture details (Redis TTL 1800, last_accessed_at) omitted entirely
ACCOUNT_SUSPENDED is mentioned but only in passing context, not preserved as a formal AC

agent_output_excerpt:
**ESCALATION — Contradiction detected. Cannot produce handoff brief.**

---

Bird and MJ use different terms for the restricted account state, and the mismatch is load-bearing:

| Agent | Term used | Where it appears |
|---|---|---|
| Bird | `suspended` | Domain rule: "a suspended account cannot initiate a new session"; AC2 error code: `ACCOUNT_SUSPENDED` |
| MJ | `locked` | Architecture: "accounts with status 'locked' will be rejected at the token issuance step" |

This is not a cosmetic naming
